foundation.label: Foundation Quiz
foundation.title: The AI gap is not a side plot. It is the reason this room exists.
foundation.intro: Answer ten foundation questions, then try the two bonus questions. The score is out of 10, but the extra credit girls can still get 12/10.

questions.prompt: Why does the AI gap between men and women matter for lAIdies?
options: Because AI is a personality test
Because confidence and access gaps can become power, pay, and influence gaps
Because the topic should be watered down
Because everyone should chase every tech trend
questions.answer: Because confidence and access gaps can become power, pay, and influence gaps
questions.explain: The point is not novelty. If one group gets fluent sooner, that group gets more say in how the work changes.
questions.review: Find it in the lAIdies positioning and About section.

questions.prompt: What is the lAIdies stance on women learning AI?
options: The references are the whole lesson
Smart professionals deserve accurate, practical AI learning that is also enjoyable and memorable
AI should stay with technical teams only
Learning AI is mostly about memorizing product names
questions.answer: Smart professionals deserve accurate, practical AI learning that is also enjoyable and memorable
questions.explain: The references are a memory and joy system. They do not replace substance, and they are not remedial packaging.
questions.review: Find it in the brand promise and About section.

questions.prompt: What happens if busy women wait until AI feels perfectly clear before trying it?
options: The gap quietly gets smaller by itself
Someone else builds the habits, vocabulary, and confidence first
questions.answer: Someone else builds the habits, vocabulary, and confidence first
questions.explain: Waiting feels reasonable in a full calendar, but early comfort compounds into workplace advantage.
questions.review: Find it in the lAIdies purpose and Issue 1 starting point.

questions.prompt: Why is AI becoming part of the invisible load at work?
options: Because everyone is expected to magically know what to use, when to use it, and how to judge the output
Because the tools explain office politics perfectly
questions.answer: Because everyone is expected to magically know what to use, when to use it, and how to judge the output
questions.explain: lAIdies exists because AI fluency is becoming another quiet expectation, and quiet expectations rarely land evenly.
questions.review: Find it in the Issue 1 working summary.

questions.prompt: When women learn AI, what changes besides individual productivity?
options: They can shape workflows, ask sharper questions, challenge weak outputs, and influence how AI gets used
They become the office help desk by default
They should use AI to make every answer sound the same
questions.answer: They can shape workflows, ask sharper questions, challenge weak outputs, and influence how AI gets used
questions.explain: The larger goal is agency. Fluency helps women participate in decisions about the work, not just speed through tasks.
questions.review: Find it in the lAIdies purpose and community framing.

questions.prompt: What does lAIdies reject as the price of AI fluency?
options: Having to become a totally different technical identity before participating
questions.answer: Having to become a totally different technical identity before participating
questions.explain: The bar is not becoming someone else. The bar is enough fluency to use the tools, evaluate the output, and stay in the conversation.
questions.review: Find it in Issue 1 and the About section.

questions.prompt: Why does lAIdies make the first AI moves small?
options: Small moves make confidence real enough to repeat
Small moves mean the topic is not serious
Small moves avoid learning anything practical
Small moves are only for beginners forever
questions.answer: Small moves make confidence real enough to repeat
questions.explain: The confidence loop starts with usable experience: try, compare, notice what works, keep judgment, and try again.
questions.review: Find it in the Issue 1 Fun Pack Try-On.

questions.prompt: Why is community part of closing the AI gap?
options: Because private confusion becomes shared vocabulary, examples, receipts, and shortcuts
Because AI works only when discussed publicly
Because women should outsource judgment to the group
questions.answer: Because private confusion becomes shared vocabulary, examples, receipts, and shortcuts
questions.explain: A room changes the learning curve. People move faster when they can trade questions, warnings, examples, and wins.
questions.review: Find it in the community section.

questions.prompt: What must stay with the human as AI becomes more useful?
options: A fear of clicking the wrong button
The belief that polished output is always right
questions.answer: Judgment, standards, context, and accountability
questions.explain: Fluency is not blind trust. It is knowing how to use help without handing over your name, standards, or responsibility.
questions.review: Find it in Issue 1's core lesson.

questions.prompt: What is one foundational reason lAIdies was created?
options: To make AI fluency practical, credible, and culturally alive for women before the gap gets wider
To replace source checking with confidence
To prove one AI tool is always best
questions.answer: To make AI fluency practical, credible, and culturally alive for women before the gap gets wider
questions.explain: The mission is practical fluency with taste and standards: useful enough for work, memorable enough to stick, and welcoming enough to start.
questions.review: Find it in the site promise and Issue 1.

questions.prompt: Bonus: why does representation in AI use and decision-making matter?
options: The people who use, question, and shape the tools influence what gets automated, trusted, funded, and normalized
Only engineers can shape AI outcomes
The tools are neutral enough that workplace context does not matter
questions.answer: The people who use, question, and shape the tools influence what gets automated, trusted, funded, and normalized
questions.explain: AI adoption is not just personal productivity. It affects whose standards and assumptions get built into future workflows.
questions.review: Find it in the lAIdies purpose and community framing.

questions.prompt: Bonus: what happens when women build real AI fluency together?
options: They become harder to sideline in conversations about the future of work
They never need expert advice again
They should accept every AI answer faster
They turn every task into a tech project
questions.answer: They become harder to sideline in conversations about the future of work
questions.explain: Shared fluency changes posture: more questions, better standards, stronger participation, and less waiting for permission.
questions.review: Find it in the lAIdies mission and community framing.

issue01.label: Issue 01 Quiz
issue01.optionLabel: Issue 01: On Wednesdays We Use AI
issue01.title: Opening the tab without making it a whole personality crisis.
issue01.intro: Answer ten Issue 1 questions, then try the two bonus questions from next week's locker. A perfect 12/10 gets two stickers because we are not monsters.

questions.prompt: What is the actual first move in Issue 1?
options: Open the tab and try one small thing
Wait until your company has a perfect AI strategy
Pretend Cher's closet computer never happened
questions.answer: Open the tab and try one small thing
questions.explain: Issue 1 is about starting small. Ten minutes beats waiting for a mythical free weekend.
questions.review: Find it in the opening and the Issue 1 Fun Pack Try-On.

questions.prompt: Why does AI feel urgent for busy corporate women?
options: Because everyone needs to become a developer
Because it is being added to the invisible load
questions.answer: Because it is being added to the invisible load
questions.explain: The point is not chasing hype. The point is not letting another workplace expectation land on you without support.
questions.review: Find it in the Working Summary and the opening argument.

questions.prompt: lAIdies is not asking you to become:
options: The IT department in better shoes
questions.answer: The IT department in better shoes
questions.explain: You can learn enough technical context to stop feeling locked out without turning into the help desk.
questions.review: Find it in the section about what lAIdies is and is not.

questions.prompt: What makes generative AI different from regular search?
options: It can create a new first pass from your instructions
questions.answer: It can create a new first pass from your instructions
questions.explain: Issue 1 frames AI as something that can draft, summarize, compare, translate tone, and organize, not just search.
questions.review: Find it in the Core Lesson.

questions.prompt: What does Cher's closet computer prove?
options: Fashion is more important than work
Useful technology can feel fun and practical
You need a Beverly Hills budget to start
questions.answer: Useful technology can feel fun and practical
questions.explain: The reference works because it makes the tech feel less sterile and more obviously useful.
questions.review: Find it in the reference discussion around Cher's closet.

questions.prompt: What does 'small sips, big moves' mean here?
options: Only learn AI at happy hour
Try one manageable move instead of waiting to feel ready
questions.answer: Try one manageable move instead of waiting to feel ready
questions.explain: The series is meant to make AI practice small enough that people actually do it.
questions.review: Find it in the Issue 1 Fun Pack Try-On and issue promise.

questions.prompt: Why does Issue 1 ask you to try the same prompt in ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini?
options: To see which tool feels most useful for the same low-risk task
To prove one tool is always best
questions.answer: To see which tool feels most useful for the same low-risk task
questions.explain: Same ask, different tools, better comparison. It is a try-on, not a final exam.
questions.review: Find it in the Issue 1 Fun Pack Try-On.

questions.prompt: What should you keep even when AI helps?
questions.answer: Your judgment
questions.explain: AI can draft, summarize, and organize. You still decide what is right, useful, and worth sending.
questions.review: Find it in the Core Lesson.

questions.prompt: What kind of first task belongs in the Issue 1 try-on?
options: A low-risk work explanation or draft you can judge
A customer commitment with no review
A company policy you will never check
questions.answer: A low-risk work explanation or draft you can judge
questions.explain: The first try should be useful, small, and safe enough that you can learn without handing it the car keys.
questions.review: Find it in the Issue 1 Fun Pack Try-On.

questions.prompt: Why does the room matter?
options: Because AI works better when lonely
Because no one should ever ask a question publicly
Because learning is easier when women trade questions, receipts, and shortcuts
Because every answer belongs in a private notebook
questions.answer: Because learning is easier when women trade questions, receipts, and shortcuts
questions.explain: The community piece matters because the questions, wins, and messy attempts are useful to more than one person.
questions.review: Find it in the community section and Website Modules.

questions.prompt: Bonus: next week, prompting gets compared to what?
options: A search bar wearing lip gloss
questions.answer: Delegating with context
questions.explain: Issue 2 is about prompting as delegation: task, audience, context, standards, and constraints.
questions.review: Preview Issue 2 when you are ready.

questions.prompt: Bonus: who becomes the patron saint of specificity in Issue 2?
questions.answer: David Rose
questions.explain: David Rose is the reminder that specific is not fussy. Specific is useful.
questions.review: Preview Issue 2's David Rose section.

issue02.label: Issue 02 Quiz
issue02.optionLabel: Issue 02: Tell Me What You Want
issue02.title: Tell me what you want, but make it painfully specific.
issue02.intro: Answer ten Issue 2 questions, then try the two Burn Book bonus questions. The score is out of 10, but the extra credit girls can still get 12/10.

questions.prompt: What usually happens when you give AI a vague ask?
options: It automatically knows your office politics
questions.answer: It gives you vague output
questions.explain: Issue 2 is the Spice Girls lesson: tell it what you want, not what you hope it guesses.
questions.review: Find it in the opening and 'Tell Me What You Want' section.

questions.prompt: Prompting is closer to:
questions.answer: Delegating with context
questions.explain: A good prompt is a brief: task, audience, context, standards, constraints, and what good looks like.
questions.review: Find it in the Episode Promise and David Rose section.

questions.prompt: Why is David Rose useful for prompting?
options: He reminds us to be very specific
He only works for fashion prompts
questions.answer: He reminds us to be very specific
questions.explain: The David Rose move is not being fussy for sport. It is giving enough detail that the output has a chance.
questions.review: Find it in The David Rose School of Prompting.

questions.prompt: What does the drawing-game analogy show?
options: The person drawing can only use the details you give them
Prompting is mostly about being cute
questions.answer: The person drawing can only use the details you give them
questions.explain: The better the description, the more recognizable the result.
questions.review: Find it in 'Tell Me What You Want, What You Really Really Want.'

questions.prompt: Which detail belongs in a stronger prompt?
options: Audience, tone, length, and what not to include
questions.answer: Audience, tone, length, and what not to include
questions.explain: Context is the difference between generic beige output and something you might actually send.
questions.review: Find it in The David Rose School of Prompting.

questions.prompt: What is the 'fold in the cheese' lesson?
options: If your instruction is unclear, AI may try but still miss what you meant
questions.answer: If your instruction is unclear, AI may try but still miss what you meant
questions.explain: The tool cannot execute the standard you did not explain.
questions.review: Find it in The David Rose School of Prompting.

questions.prompt: If the first AI output is bad, what is the smarter next move?
options: Improve the ask and try again
questions.answer: Improve the ask and try again
questions.explain: Bad first output is often feedback on the prompt, not proof that you are doomed.
questions.review: Find it in Same Task, Better Ask.

questions.prompt: Why tell AI what not to include?
options: It sets boundaries before the draft wanders off
It turns the prompt into code
questions.answer: It sets boundaries before the draft wanders off
questions.explain: Constraints are useful. They keep the output from adding fluff, fake confidence, or a tone you would never use.
questions.review: Find it in The David Rose School of Prompting.

questions.prompt: What does the Harvard/BCG research note warn us about?
options: AI can help inside its strengths, but polished wrong answers still need judgment
questions.answer: AI can help inside its strengths, but polished wrong answers still need judgment
questions.explain: The lesson is not blind trust. It is context, judgment, and verification.
questions.review: Find it in the Research Note.

questions.prompt: What was the Issue 2 try-on?
options: Give AI the same real task twice: once vague, once specific
Ask AI to pick your outfit and call it strategy
questions.answer: Give AI the same real task twice: once vague, once specific
questions.explain: The point is to see the difference. Same task, better brief, much better odds.
questions.review: Find it in the Issue 2 Fun Pack Try-On.

questions.prompt: Bonus: next week's Burn Book issue is about what AI problem?
questions.answer: AI hallucinations
questions.explain: Issue 3 is about confident output that may be wrong, unsupported, outdated, or made up.
questions.review: Preview the next issue once it drops.

questions.prompt: Bonus: what should you ask for before trusting high-stakes AI output?
questions.answer: Receipts
questions.explain: If the claim matters, the source matters. Confidence is not a citation.
questions.review: Preview the next issue once it drops.
